Document Title: Deep Learning-based Echocardiography Enhancement: Reducing Speckle Noise and Improving Temporal Resolution
Authors: G. Carcedo-Rodríguez; B. Vazquez; J. Perez-Gonzalez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Author Affiliations: Master Program in Computer Science and Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Ultrasound is widely used in medical imaging due to its accessibility and cost-effectiveness. This paper presents a Deep Learning-based approach for enhancing echocardiographic imaging by addressing two challenges: speckle noise reduction and temporal resolution improvement. For denoising task, we propose the SE-U-Net model, while for temporal enhancement we employ Real-Time Intermediate Flow Estimation for frame interpolation. Multiple datasets were used for training, validation, and testing of both models. The proposed pipeline was applied in high heart rate murine models to enhance relevant information. In final evaluation, the denoising model achieved SSIM=0.99 and PSNR=38.27dB, indicating effective contrast enhancement, preservation of homogeneous regions, and reduction of small artifacts. The interpolation model, on the other hand, achieved SSIM=0.87 and PSNR=33.23dB, enabling the increase of ultrasound temporal resolution from 37 to 296 frames per second without losing image quality.

Document Title: Convolutional Neural Network Trained with Heatmaps for Segmentation and Measurement of the Femur in Fetal Ultrasound
Authors: J. C. A. Ortíz; F. A. Cosío; J. Pérez-González; N. O. C. Díaz; R. V. Cristerna; V. M. Bañuelos; L. C. Marín; M. G. Huerta
Author Affiliations: Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de Obstetricia, Instituto Nacional de Perinatología, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de Obstetricia, Instituto Nacional de Perinatología, Ciudad de México, México
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Fetal femur length measurement via ultrasound, alongside abdominal circumference, biparietal diameter, and cranial circumference assessments, are key components of prenatal screening, as they enable the estimation of fetal weight and the evaluation of intrauterine development. Currently, this procedure still heavily relies on manual intervention by expert radiologists or sonographers, who must identify anatomical landmarks to perform the measurements. This dependence on specialized human expertise limits diagnostic accessibility in remote areas far from major urban centers. In this work, we propose a novel convolutional neural network trained on binary masks and heatmaps of femur endpoints. The model achieves robust performance, segmenting the femur with an 88% Dice coefficient, and measuring its length with only a 2.515% error margin. This work is part of a broader machine learning based system for fetal weight estimation, aiming to expand diagnostic capabilities to healthcare centers with limited human resources.
IEEE Terms: Heating systems;Ultrasonic imaging;Ultrasonic variables measurement;Measurement uncertainty;Estimation;Medical services;Manuals;Biomedical measurement;Length measurement;Convolutional neural networks

Document Title: Keep it Simple: Understanding Natural Language Commands for General-Purpose Service Robots
Authors: S. Ortuno-Chanelo; L. Contreras; J. Savage; H. Okada
Author Affiliations: Biorobotics Laboratory, School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico; Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Tamagawa University; Biorobotics Laboratory, School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico; Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Tamagawa University
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE/SICE International Symposium on System Integration (SII)
Abstract: Service robots are designed to perform useful tasks for humans, which involve managing and combining a variety of skills in the form of global and local plans to solve any given task. In this work, we propose a framework to process natural language commands for general-purpose service robots where the robot should perform an arbitrary spoken command requested by a non-expert operator. Our system uses a Natural Language Processing (NLP) parser and a Conceptual Dependency (CD) builder to create CD structures that an expert system can employ to generate a global plan that the robot will execute. An extra simplification step using Large Language Models (LLM) was tested and evaluated in order to improve the accuracy of the system. Finally, our system has been tested in challenging environments in robot competitions and we have achieved promising results.
IEEE Terms: Service robots;System integration;Syntactics;Natural language processing;Generators;Task analysis;Expert systems

Document Title: Detection of Optimal Echocardiographic Video Segments Using Deep Learning
Authors: I. Perez-Ruiz; J. Perez-Gonzalez; A. Carrera-Pinzón; R. E.; R. H.; B. Y.; A. C. F.
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: The identification of optimal echocardiographic video segments is a critical initial step for reliable cardiac function assessment. This study focuses on the development and evaluation of deep learning models for the automated classification of correct versus incorrect echocardiographic planes in a video sequence, specifically for Subcostal Four-Chamber (S4C) views. A dataset comprising 10 S4C studies was used, from which frames were extracted and preprocessed. Preprocessing included metadata removal, resizing to 224×224 pixels, and data augmentation. The resulting images were divided into training (2068 images per class), validation (517 images per class), and test (200 images per class) sets. Two convolutional neural network architectures, VGG-16 and Vision Transformer (ViT), were trained and evaluated. The VGG-16 model, trained for 30 epochs, achieved a test accuracy of 0.98 and an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 1.00. The Vision Transformer, trained for 30 epochs, yielded a test accuracy of 0.89 and an AUC of 0.97. These preliminary results demonstrate the potential of deep learning models, particularly VGG-16, to accurately identify optimal frames in echocardiographic video sequences.
